text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known stage IV melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, on targeted therapy (Tafinlar/tram). Restaging evaluation. Comparison made to prior CT abdomen/pelvis dated approximately 3 months ago.

---

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of IV iohexol contrast. Oral contrast was administered. Images acquired in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformations generated and reviewed.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs: No new pulmonary nodules identified. Scattered sub-5mm nodules in the right lower lobe, unchanged and felt to be indeterminate/likely benign given stability. No consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Airways patent to the subsegmental level.
Mediastinum/Hila: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Cardiomediastinal silhouette unremarkable. No pericardial effusion.
Bony thorax: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: Multiple hepatic lesions again identified, overall appearing stable to minimally enlarged compared to the 3-month scan. The largest lesion, in hepatic segment VII, measures approximately 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension — previously measured at 1.1 cm. The remaining lesions are scattered throughout both lobes, the majority measuring less than 1 cm, and are largely similar in size to prior. There is no new hepatic lesion identified. Enhancement pattern remains heterogeneous, without frank necrosis. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.
Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal lesion.
Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation.
Adrenal glands: No abnormality identified bilaterally.
Kidneys: Normal enhancement bilaterally. No hydronephrosis.
Gastrointestinal tract: No obstruction. No bowel wall thickening.
Peritoneum/Ascites: No ascites. No obvious peritoneal nodularity.

PELVIS:
Right inguinal region: A right inguinal lymph node measures 1.1 cm in short axis — previously 0.8 cm. This represents a slight increase in size compared to prior, though still within a range where clinical correlation is warranted. Left inguinal nodes unremarkable.
Pelvic organs: Uterus and adnexa appear within normal limits. No pelvic free fluid.
Bladder: Unremarkable.

SOFT TISSUE / SUBCUTANEOUS:
No discrete subcutaneous nodules identified. The previously noted subcutaneous soft tissue deposits are no longer visualized, consistent with continued resolution.

SKELETON:
No new suspicious osseous lesions. Previously noted incidental finding of mild degenerative changes at the lower lumbar spine, stable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Hepatic metastatic disease: The majority of hepatic lesions appear stable compared to the 3-month study. The dominant segment VII lesion is minimally enlarged at 1.4 cm (previously 1.1 cm). Overall, the prior dramatic reduction in hepatic burden appears to have plateaued, with the current scan suggesting early stabilization rather than continued regression.

2. Right inguinal lymphadenopathy: Slight interval increase in the right inguinal node (1.1 cm vs. 0.8 cm). Difficult to characterize definitively — could represent early nodal re-involvement versus reactive change. Warrants attention on next scan.

3. Subcutaneous disease: Not identified on this examination, in keeping with the prior near-complete resolution.

4. No new intrathoracic or intraperitoneal sites of disease.

Overall, the dramatic response seen through the 3-month timepoint appears to have stabilized. The degree of response is less pronounced compared to that earlier assessment, with modest changes in the dominant hepatic lesion and one inguinal node. LDH reported separately by the ordering team.

Signed: [Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging]
